questions.question: Explain the concept of locality of reference in memory hierarchy design. Distinguish between temporal and spatial locality with suitable examples, and explain how these principles influence cache performance.
keyPoints: Temporal locality: recently accessed items likely to be accessed again (loops, variables)
Spatial locality: items near recently accessed location likely to be accessed soon (sequential code, arrays)
Determines cache effectiveness and block size selection
Influences replacement policies and prefetching strategies

questions.question: Differentiate between write-through and write-back policies for cache memory. Explain the role of a write buffer in cache systems and discuss the advantages and disadvantages of each policy.
keyPoints: Write-through: writes to both cache and main memory simultaneously
Write-back: writes only to cache, main memory updated later (dirty bit)
Write buffer: temporary storage to hide memory write latency
Trade-off: write-back faster but complex; write-through simpler but slower

questions.question: A computer system has a main memory of 64 KB with word size 1 byte. The cache is 4 KB in size and uses direct mapping with a block size of 8 bytes. For the following memory address sequence (in hexadecimal): 0x0A24, 0x0A28, 0x0B30, 0x0A20, 0x0B38 — (i) Show how the main memory address is divided into tag, block index, and offset fields. (ii) Determine whether each memory reference results in a cache hit or miss. (iii) Calculate the hit rate for this sequence. (iv) If the cache is changed to 2-way set-associative with LRU replacement, recalculate the hit/miss status for the same address sequence.
keyPoints: Address format: 16-bit address, 3-bit offset (8 bytes/block), 6-bit block index (64 blocks), 7-bit tag
Direct mapping: block address modulo number of cache blocks determines cache location
Hit/miss analysis tracks cache state after each reference with proper replacement
Set-associative: 32 sets, each with 2 lines; LRU policy affects replacement decisions

questions.question: (i) Explain the working of a virtually indexed physically tagged (VIPT) cache with a 4 KB page size. Discuss the issue of aliasing and how it can be resolved. (ii) A system has auxiliary memory with average seek time of 10 ms, rotational delay of 5 ms, and transfer rate of 4 MB/s. Calculate the average access time for reading a 1 KB block. (iii) Explain the concept of virtual memory and discuss how page replacement algorithms (FIFO, LRU, Optimal) differ in their approach to page faults. (iv) Describe the hierarchical structure of storage in a computer system from registers to magnetic tape, highlighting the trade-offs between speed, capacity, and cost per bit.
keyPoints: VIPT: uses page offset for cache index (avoids translation for hit determination), uses physical tag for comparison; aliasing solved by cache size ≤ page size × associativity
Auxiliary memory: average access time = seek + rotational delay + transfer time = 10 + 5 + (1KB/4MB) = 15.25 ms
Virtual memory: provides illusion of larger memory via paging; page replacement algorithms minimize faults using different strategies (queue, recency, oracle)
Storage hierarchy: registers (ns, KB) → cache (ns, MB) → main memory (μs, GB) → SSD (μs-ms, TB) → HDD/hybrid (ms, TB) → tape (s, PB)
